name: Auschwitz-Birkenau
note: Largest Nazi concentration and extermination camp in occupied Poland.

name: Treblinka
note: Extermination camp in occupied Poland where ~870,000 Jews were murdered.

name: Sobibor
note: Extermination camp in occupied Poland, site of prisoner uprising in 1943.

name: Belzec
note: First Operation Reinhard extermination camp; ~500,000 Jews killed.

name: Chelmno (Kulmhof)
note: First Nazi extermination camp, where victims were murdered in gas vans.

name: Majdanek (Lublin)
note: Concentration and extermination camp near Lublin, Poland.

name: Bergen-Belsen
note: Concentration camp in Germany, site of mass deaths from starvation and disease.

name: Dachau
note: First Nazi concentration camp, near Munich, Germany.

name: Sachsenhausen
note: Concentration camp north of Berlin, used as a model for other camps.

name: Ravensbrück
note: Camp primarily for women, located in northern Germany.

name: Mauthausen
note: Austrian camp notorious for forced labor in quarries.

name: Theresienstadt (Terezín)
note: Czech ghetto-camp used for propaganda and as a transit camp.

name: Warsaw Ghetto Deportations
note: Mass deportations from Warsaw Ghetto to Treblinka during Grossaktion Warsaw (approx. 265,000 Jews).

name: Łódź Ghetto Deportations
note: Deportations from Łódź (Litzmannstadt) Ghetto primarily to Chełmno extermination camp and later to Auschwitz.

name: Vilna Ghetto Deportations
note: Deportations from Vilna to Ponary forest, site of mass shootings where ~70,000 Jews were murdered.

name: Theresienstadt Deportations
note: Deportations from Theresienstadt to Auschwitz; many elderly Jews sent to Theresienstadt as 'model ghetto' before extermination.

name: Kovno Ghetto Deportations
note: Deportations from Kovno (Kaunas) Ghetto to Ninth Fort and later Auschwitz. Thousands murdered at Ninth Fort shootings.

name: Salonika Deportations
note: Jews of Thessaloniki (Salonika, Greece) deported to Auschwitz. Around 45,000–50,000 murdered upon arrival.

name: Amsterdam Deportations
note: Deportations from Amsterdam via Westerbork transit camp to Auschwitz and Sobibor. Anne Frank among those deported.

name: Paris Drancy Deportations
note: Deportations from Drancy transit camp near Paris to Auschwitz. Over 67,000 French Jews deported.

name: Budapest Deportations
note: Deportations of Hungarian Jews in 1944, primarily to Auschwitz; over 400,000 Jews deported in just two months.

name: Prague Deportations
note: Deportations of Prague Jews via Theresienstadt, then onward to Auschwitz and other extermination camps.

name: Boycott of all Jewish Shops
note: Boycott of all Jewish shops in Berlin. In October, all hospitals declared 'free' of Jewish doctors.

name: Warsaw Ghetto
note: The Warsaw Ghetto was established and liquidated during WWII.

name: Prague
note: 18,000 (8% of the population

name: Odesa Pogrom
note: One of the earliest pogroms; Jews accused of involvement in the death of the Greek Orthodox patriarch.

name: Odesa Pogroms
note: A series of violent attacks on Jews in Odesa, including the major 1871 pogrom.

name: Kiev Pogrom
note: Massive pogrom following assassination of Tsar Alexander II; hundreds of Jewish homes and shops destroyed.

name: Warsaw Pogrom
note: Anti-Jewish riot in Warsaw, then part of the Russian Empire.

name: Balta Pogrom
note: Pogrom in Podolia Governorate, causing mass destruction of Jewish property.

name: Chişinău Pogrom
note: Also known as the Kishinev Pogrom; 49 Jews killed, hundreds wounded, property destroyed.

name: Homel Pogrom
note: Anti-Jewish violence in Homel (now Belarus) after disputes with Russian nationalists.

name: Białystok Pogrom
note: More than 80 Jews murdered; instigated by local Russian authorities.

name: Kiev Pogroms
note: During Russian Civil War, thousands of Jews murdered in Ukraine in a wave of pogroms by various armies.

name: Lwów Pogrom
note: Following Polish takeover of Lwów, hundreds of Jews killed, synagogues and homes burned.

name: Hebron Massacre
note: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1929_Hebron_massacre

name: Warsaw Ghetto
note: The Warsaw Ghetto was established and liquidated during WWII.

name: כסף נבחר R' Yoshiyahu Pinto
note: A collection of d'rashot on Bereishit and Shemot.

name: כסף מזוקק R' Yoshiyahu Pinto
note: A homiletic commentary on the Torah, followed by a pamphlet entitled Kesef To'afot, glosses on the Torah

name: מאור עיניים R' Yoshiyahu Pinto
note: A commentary on R'Yakov ibn Habib's Ein Yaakov on the Agaddah sections of the Gemara

name: R' Yoshiyahu Pinto postumous sefarim and works
note:
• כסף צורף – commentary on Mishle
  • נבחר מכסף – Responsa
  • כסף נמס– commentary on Eichah
  • קבוצת כסף - works on halacha

name: 12000 Jewish German soldiers die
note: Of 100,000 Jews serving in the German army, 12000 die in the first world war.

name: First Aliyah
note: Wave of Jewish immigration to Ottoman Palestine, mostly from Eastern Europe and Yemen; established agricultural settlements like Rishon LeZion, Petah Tikva, and Zikhron Yaakov.

name: Publication of Der Judenstaat
note: Theodor Herzl published 'Der Judenstaat' in Vienna, laying the ideological foundation for political Zionism.

name: First Zionist Congress
note: Held in Basel. Theodor Herzl convened the first congress, established the World Zionist Organization, adopted the Basel Program.

name: Second Zionist Congress
note: Basel. Established the Jewish Colonial Trust, the financial arm of the Zionist movement.

name: Third Zionist Congress
note: Basel. Focused on organizational matters and support for settlement activity in Palestine.

name: Fourth Zionist Congress
note: Basel. Known as the 'Culture Congress.' Debated the role of Hebrew culture and education in Zionism.

name: Fifth Zionist Congress
note: Basel. Created the Jewish National Fund (Keren Kayemet LeYisrael) to purchase land in Palestine.

name: Sixth Zionist Congress
note: Basel. The 'Uganda Proposal' for temporary Jewish settlement in East Africa was presented, causing heated controversy.

name: Second Aliyah
note: Immigration wave bringing socialist pioneers from Eastern Europe; foundation of the kibbutz movement and Hashomer defense organization.

name: Seventh Zionist Congress
note: Basel. Uganda Proposal officially rejected; movement reaffirmed Palestine as the goal.

name: Eighth Zionist Congress
note: Basel. Marked shift to practical Zionism, focusing on settlement and Hebrew education.

name: Ninth Zionist Congress
note: Basel. Chaim Weizmann began to rise as a leading Zionist figure.

name: Tel Aviv Founded
note: Jewish neighborhood Ahuzat Bayit established outside Jaffa, later becoming Tel Aviv.

name: Tenth Zionist Congress
note: Basel. Continued debates between political and practical Zionists.

name: Eleventh Zionist Congress
note: Basel. Last congress before World War I; debates on education and Hebrew culture.

name: Balfour Declaration
note: British Foreign Secretary Arthur Balfour declared support for a 'national home for the Jewish people' in Palestine.

name: San Remo Conference
note: Allied powers assigned the Mandate for Palestine to Britain, incorporating the Balfour Declaration.

name: League of Nations Mandate for Palestine
note: League of Nations formally approved the British Mandate for Palestine, recognizing Jewish national aspirations.

name: Twelfth Zionist Congress
note: Carlsbad. First post–WWI congress; endorsed Balfour Declaration and British Mandate.

name: Thirteenth Zionist Congress
note: Carlsbad. Chaim Weizmann emphasized practical work in Palestine; tensions grew with Revisionist Zionists.

name: Fourteenth Zionist Congress
note: Basel. Marked the inauguration of Hebrew University in Jerusalem. Weizmann re-elected President.

name: Hebrew University Opened
note: Inauguration of Hebrew University on Mount Scopus, Jerusalem, attended by Chaim Weizmann and Lord Balfour.

name: Fifteenth Zionist Congress
note: Basel. Discussed economic challenges facing the Yishuv and immigration.

name: Sixteenth Zionist Congress
note: Basel. Debates following Arab riots in Palestine (Hebron massacre).

name: Seventeenth Zionist Congress
note: Basel. Zionist Revisionists under Jabotinsky walked out; Weizmann resigned as President.

name: Eighteenth Zionist Congress
note: Basel. First after Nazi rise to power in Germany. Focused on rescue and immigration.

name: Nineteenth Zionist Congress
note: Basel. Chaim Weizmann returned as leader. Emphasis on Jewish refugee crisis in Europe.

name: Twentieth Zionist Congress
note: Basel. Debated the British Peel Commission proposal for partition of Palestine. Movement split on acceptance.

name: Twenty-First Zionist Congress
note: Basel. Opposed British 1939 White Paper restricting Jewish immigration, especially as Holocaust began.

name: White Paper of 1939
note: British policy severely limiting Jewish immigration to Palestine on the eve of the Holocaust.

name: Twenty-Second Zionist Congress
note: Basel. First after the Holocaust. Declared demand for a Jewish state in Palestine.
